flashcards.back: Time complexity measures the amount of time an algorithm takes to complete as a function of input size. It describes the upper bound on the number of primitive operations executed, typically expressed using asymptotic notation.

flashcards.front: Define Big O (O), Omega (Ω), and Theta (Θ) notations
flashcards.back: Big O (O) gives an upper bound (worst case), Omega (Ω) gives a lower bound (best case), and Theta (Θ) gives a tight bound when upper and lower bounds are the same. O(f(n)) ≤ c·f(n), Ω(f(n)) ≥ c·f(n), Θ(f(n)) = c₁·f(n) to c₂·f(n).

flashcards.front: How do you analyze nested loops in time complexity?
flashcards.back: Multiply the complexity of each loop level. For n iterations in an outer loop and m iterations in an inner loop, complexity is O(n × m). If both are n, it's O(n²). Logarithmic loops (while i < n { i *= 2 }) give O(log n).

flashcards.front: List common time complexities from fastest to slowest
flashcards.back: O(1) < O(log n) < O(n) < O(n log n) < O(n²) < O(n³) < O(2^n) < O(n!). Examples: O(1) - array access; O(log n) - binary search; O(n) - linear search; O(n log n) - merge sort; O(n²) - bubble sort; O(2^n) - recursive Fibonacci.

flashcards.front: Explain Best, Average, and Worst Case time complexity
flashcards.back: Best Case (Ω): minimum time for favorable input (e.g., linear search finds target at first position O(1)). Average Case (Θ): expected time with random input distribution. Worst Case (O): maximum time for least favorable input (e.g., linear search finds target at last position O(n)).

flashcards.front: What is a Recurrence Relation in algorithm analysis?
flashcards.back: A recurrence relation expresses the time complexity T(n) of a recursive algorithm in terms of smaller inputs. Example: T(n) = T(n-1) + O(1) solves to O(n). T(n) = 2T(n/2) + O(n) solves to O(n log n) using the Master Theorem.

flashcards.front: State the Master Theorem for divide-and-conquer recurrences
flashcards.back: For recurrence T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n) where a≥1, b>1: If f(n) = O(n^(log_b a - ε)), then T(n) = Θ(n^(log_b a)). If f(n) = Θ(n^(log_b a)), then T(n) = Θ(n^(log_b a) log n). If f(n) = Ω(n^(log_b a + ε)), then T(n) = Θ(f(n)).

flashcards.front: Compare O(n!) and O(2^n) time complexities
flashcards.back: Both are exponential and impractical for large n. O(2^n) grows slower than O(n!) since n! = n×(n-1)×...×2×1 > 2^n for n≥4. For n=20: 2^n ≈ 1 million, 20! ≈ 2.4×10^18. Algorithms generating all permutations typically have O(n!) complexity.
